Masayoshi Son, the multibillionaire founder of
Japanese investment company SoftBank, is one of the most intriguing, if utt=
erly mysterious, businessmen on the world stage. He rarely gives interviews=
 but is prone to grandiose pronouncements and even more grandiose investmen=
ts=E2=80=94some of which have gone completely off the rails (WeWork), and o=
thers that have worked spectacularly well (Alibaba). But with SoftBank repo=
rtedly considering a staggering investment of between $15 billion and $25 b=
illion in OpenAI=E2=80=94in addition to his recent
appearance with President Trump, Larry Ellison, and Sam Altman promoting a =
plan to invest $500 billion in U.S. A.I. infrastructure=E2=80=94Masa is sud=
denly very much in the news.

As luck would have it, Son is also the subject of not one but two new books=
: The Money Trap: Lost Illusions Inside the Tech Bubble, by Alok Sama, the =
former
president and C.F.O. of SoftBank; and Gambling Man, by Lionel Barber, the d=
istinguished former editor of the Financial Times, who recently discussed h=
is reporting at the Economic Club of New York. In conversation with Glenn H=
ubbard, the former dean of Columbia Business School and chairman of the Cou=
ncil of Economic Advisers under George W. Bush, Barber explained that he de=
cided to write the
book because of one word: money. Masa has =E2=80=9Cliterally made or lost h=
undreds of billions of dollars,=E2=80=9D Barber said. =E2=80=9CHe was the r=
ichest man in the world for three days at the height of the dot-com bubble,=
 in February 2000, and in my experience in journalism: Follow the money. If=
 there=E2=80=99s money around, there=E2=80=99s got to be a lot of good stor=
ies.=E2=80=9D I certainly agree.

Tokens in the Legend Meter
--------------------------

Despite his achievements, Masa remains an =E2=80=9Coutsider=E2=80=9D among =
the
corporate elite in Japan. For starters, he=E2=80=99s Korean, not Japanese. =
His grandfather came to Japan in 1917, and his grandmother a decade later. =
=E2=80=9CThey were immigrants,=E2=80=9D Barber said=E2=80=94economic migran=
ts who took Japanese names to fit in, =E2=80=9Cliterally fourth-class citiz=
ens.=E2=80=9D His grandparents went back to Korea during World War II, then=
 returned to Japan and spent the next decade destitute, with Masa=E2=80=99s=
 father engaging in bootlegging, loan sharking, and then Pachinko. But by t=
he 1960s, amid the postwar
=E2=80=9CJapanese economic miracle,=E2=80=9D Masa=E2=80=99s family was =E2=
=80=9Cquite well off,=E2=80=9D Barber said, =E2=80=9Calthough Masa likes to=
 tell everybody that he grew up in a slum and he was really poor. That was =
actually quite a short time.=E2=80=9D

The turning point for Masa was when he decided to finish high school in the=
 United States, and then continued on to college at Berkeley. He learned En=
glish; he educated himself about the microchip and the burgeoning technolog=
y revolution; and
upon his return to Japan, he built a software distribution business, becomi=
ng a =E2=80=9Cmiddleman=E2=80=9D between American technology companies and =
the Japanese market. He was close to Steve Jobs, which won him the exclusiv=
e right to distribute the iPhone in Japan. He cozied up to Bill Gates and B=
obby Kotick. =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98You want to get into the mass consumer marke=
t in Japan,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D he told them, per Barber. =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98I=
=E2=80=99m the guy.
I can unlock the black box of distribution. I can be your cultural interpre=
ter.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Then he started making bets of his own. He met Jack Ma in 1999 and made a b=
ig, early, $100 million investment in Alibaba. (He stuck with the company e=
ven after the dot-com crash, and made a fortune.) He made another big, earl=
y bet on Yahoo. And yet another big, early bet on
mobile phone technology, buying Sprint in the U.S. and then merging it with=
 T-Mobile U.S. to create a viable competitor to Verizon and AT&T. In 2016, =
he bought Arm Holdings, =E2=80=9CBritain=E2=80=99s best technology company,=
=E2=80=9D Barber said, for $30 billion. Arm is now worth around $140 billio=
n, and it=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cthe platform for all [Son=E2=80=99s] ambitions=
 in the A.I. space.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

In closing this last deal, Masa sought to meet Arm=E2=80=99s chairman on a =
yacht in the Bosporus, and planned to show up as he
did to other maritime billionaire t=C3=AAte-=C3=A0-t=C3=AAtes=E2=80=94by la=
nding his helicopter on the vessel=E2=80=99s helipad. =E2=80=9CHe=E2=80=99s=
 obviously thinking it=E2=80=99s some kind of Russian oligarch=E2=80=99s su=
peryacht,=E2=80=9D Barber said. =E2=80=9CAnd the poor English guy=E2=80=99s=
 just got a sloop.=E2=80=9D They ended up meeting in Marmaris, in Turkey. =
=E2=80=9CMasa just hits him with a big cash offer, unconditional, and then =
makes two or three other offers, and that=E2=80=99s it,=E2=80=9D he said. T=
he entire sale process took two weeks.

Part of Masa=E2=80=99s strategy, Barber explained, is to

ang of Yahoo at one point, =E2=80=98You need $100 million.=E2=80=99 And Jer=
ry Yang says, =E2=80=98No, I don=E2=80=99t need $100 million.=E2=80=99 And =
Masa looks at him and says, =E2=80=98Everybody needs $100 million. And if y=

=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D

Of course, that approach can also lead to expensive failures. Masa=E2=80=99=

But he didn=E2=80=99t want to ask Masa, lest he owe a debt to the subject o=

=98You can=E2=80=99t write a word about this.=E2=80=99 I=E2=80=99m thinking=

=98But I=E2=80=99m not a journalist. I=E2=80=99m an author.=E2=80=99 Just k=
ind of cheeky, but I got away with it.=E2=80=9D

In any case, he thinks Masa liked the book well enough=E2=80=94the two ran =

=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;"><strong>Masayoshi Son</strong>, the multib=

Japanese investment company SoftBank, is one of the most intriguing, if utt=
erly mysterious, businessmen on the world stage. He rarely gives interviews=
 but is prone to grandiose pronouncements and even more grandiose investmen=
ts=E2=80=94some of which have gone completely off the rails (WeWork), and o=
thers that have worked spectacularly well (Alibaba). But with SoftBank repo=
rtedly considering a <em>staggering </em>investment of between $15 billion =
and $25 billion in OpenAI=E2=80=94in addition to his recent
appearance with President <strong>Trump</strong>, <strong>Larry</strong> <s=
trong>Ellison</strong>, and <strong>Sam</strong> <strong>Altman</strong> pr=
omoting a plan to invest $500 billion in U.S. A.I. infrastructure=E2=80=94M=
asa is suddenly very much in the news.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;"><br/>As lu=
ck would have it, Son is also the subject of not one but <em>two</em> new b=
ooks: <em>The Money Trap: Lost Illusions Inside the Tech Bubble</em>, by <s=
trong>Alok</strong> <strong>Sama</strong>, the former
president and C.F.O. of SoftBank; and <em>Gambling Man</em>, by <strong>Lio=
nel</strong> <strong>Barber</strong>, the distinguished former editor of th=
e <em>Financial Times</em>, who recently discussed his reporting at the Eco=
nomic Club of New York. In conversation with <strong>Glenn</strong> <strong=
>Hubbard</strong>, the former dean of Columbia Business School and chairman=
 of the Council of Economic Advisers under <strong>George W. Bush</strong>,=
 Barber explained that he decided to write the
book because of one word: money. Masa has =E2=80=9Cliterally made or lost h=
undreds of billions of dollars,=E2=80=9D Barber said. =E2=80=9CHe was the r=
ichest man in the world for three days at the height of the dot-com bubble,=
 in February 2000, and in my experience in journalism: Follow the money. If=
 there=E2=80=99s money around, there=E2=80=99s got to be a lot of good stor=
ies.=E2=80=9D I certainly agree.</p></div>

=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Barber=E2=80=99s path to Masa began a deca=
de ago, during the process
that led to Nikkei acquiring the <em>FT</em> for $1.3 billion, or 44x earni=
ngs=E2=80=94yes, that=E2=80=99s the real price paid for a real <em>newspape=
r</em>. He made 14 trips to Japan over four years to cement the relationshi=
p between the <em>FT</em> and its new owners, and during those trips, he sa=
id, =E2=80=9CI kept hearing about this figure, Masayoshi Son.=E2=80=9D One =
or two people at Nikkei suggested looking into him, maybe doing a =E2=80=9C=
Lunch with the FT=E2=80=9D column. But, Barber said, =E2=80=9Che was always=
 elusive.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:
0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">That continued to be=
 the case when Barber got to work on a biography. =E2=80=9CIt took a lot of=
 work,=E2=80=9D he said=E2=80=94at one point he told an Indian American inv=
estor that pursuing Masa was harder than stalking a Bengal tiger. (=E2=80=
=9C=E2=80=98In that case, Mr. Barber,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D he recalled the inv=
estor saying, =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98I would bring a goat.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D) He=
 twice traveled to Tokyo to interview his subject, but =E2=80=9Chis people =
said, =E2=80=98He=E2=80=99s too busy,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Barber remembered. =
=E2=80=9CWith Masayoshi Son, it was very
important to deliver a message: =E2=80=98Look, this isn=E2=80=99t an assass=
ination. This isn=E2=80=99t a hit. I=E2=80=99m not interested in doing cari=
catures. I=E2=80=99ve done my homework.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D</p></div>

<table class=3D"c4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspaci=
ng=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspa=
ce:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-=
bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'In=
ter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;=
line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Despite his achievements, Masa remains an =E2=
=80=9Coutsider=E2=80=9D among the
corporate elite in Japan. For starters, he=E2=80=99s Korean, not Japanese. =
His grandfather came to Japan in 1917, and his grandmother a decade later. =
=E2=80=9CThey were immigrants,=E2=80=9D Barber said=E2=80=94economic migran=
ts who took Japanese names to fit in, =E2=80=9Cliterally fourth-class citiz=
ens.=E2=80=9D His grandparents went back to Korea during World War II, then=
 returned to Japan and spent the next decade destitute, with Masa=E2=80=99s=
 father engaging in bootlegging, loan sharking, and then Pachinko. But by t=
he 1960s, amid the postwar
=E2=80=9CJapanese economic miracle,=E2=80=9D Masa=E2=80=99s family was =E2=
=80=9Cquite well off,=E2=80=9D Barber said, =E2=80=9Calthough Masa likes to=
 tell everybody that he grew up in a slum and he was really poor. That was =
actually quite a short time.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/></p><p style=3D"margin:0;mar=
gin-bottom:0px;">The turning point for Masa was when he decided to finish h=
igh school in the United States, and then continued on to college at Berkel=
ey. He learned English; he educated himself about the microchip and the bur=
geoning technology revolution; and
upon his return to Japan, he built a software distribution business, becomi=
ng a =E2=80=9Cmiddleman=E2=80=9D between American technology companies and =
the Japanese market. He was close to <strong>Steve</strong> <strong>Jobs</s=
trong>, which won him the exclusive right to distribute the iPhone in Japan=
=2E He cozied up to <strong>Bill</stron=
g> <strong>Gates</strong> and <strong>=
Bobby</strong> <strong>Kotick</strong>. =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98You want to get i=
nto the mass consumer market in Japan,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D he told them, per =
Barber. =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98I=E2=80=99m the guy.
I can unlock the black box of distribution. I can be your cultural interpre=
ter.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Then he started making b=
ets of his own. He met <strong>Jack</strong> <strong>Ma</strong> in 1999 an=
d made a big, early, $100 million investment in Alibaba. (He stuck with the=
 company even after the dot-com crash, and made a fortune.) He made another=
 big, early bet on Yahoo. And yet <em>another</em> big, early bet on
mobile phone technology, buying Sprint in the U.S. and then merging it with=
 T-Mobile U.S. to create a viable competitor to Verizon and AT&T. In 20=
16, he bought Arm Holdings, =E2=80=9CBritain=E2=80=99s best technology comp=
any,=E2=80=9D Barber said, for $30 billion. Arm is now worth around $140 bi=
llion, and it=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cthe platform for all [Son=E2=80=99s] ambit=
ions in the A.I. space.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;"><br/>In cl=
osing this last deal, Masa sought to meet Arm=E2=80=99s chairman on a yacht=
 in the Bosporus, and planned to show up as he
did to other maritime billionaire t=C3=AAte-=C3=A0-t=C3=AAtes=E2=80=94by la=
nding his helicopter on the vessel=E2=80=99s helipad. =E2=80=9CHe=E2=80=99s=
 obviously thinking it=E2=80=99s some kind of Russian oligarch=E2=80=99s su=
peryacht,=E2=80=9D Barber said. =E2=80=9CAnd the poor English guy=E2=80=99s=
 just got a sloop.=E2=80=9D They ended up meeting in Marmaris, in Turkey. =
=E2=80=9CMasa just hits him with a big cash offer, unconditional, and then =
makes two or three other offers, and that=E2=80=99s it,=E2=80=9D he said. T=
he entire sale process took two weeks.<br/><br/>Part of Masa=E2=80=99s stra=
tegy, Barber explained, is to
overwhelm founders with his financial largesse. =E2=80=9CHe says to <strong=
>Jerry</strong> <strong>Yang</strong> of Yahoo at one point, =E2=80=98You n=
eed $100 million.=E2=80=99 And Jerry Yang says, =E2=80=98No, I don=E2=80=99=
t need $100 million.=E2=80=99 And Masa looks at him and says, =E2=80=98Ever=
ybody needs $100 million. And if you don=E2=80=99t take it, I=E2=80=99m goi=
ng to give it to your competitor.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>Of course, tha=
t approach can also lead to expensive failures. Masa=E2=80=99s flood-the-zo=
ne strategy with WeWork=E2=80=99s <strong>Adam</strong> <strong>Neumann</st=
rong>
blew up spectacularly and cost him billions. Masa =E2=80=9Csuffers from wha=
t I call =E2=80=98founder syndrome,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Barber said. =E2=80=
=9CIf he meets somebody else who talks as big as he does, =E2=80=A6 and Ada=
m Neumann talks big, =E2=80=A6 Masa just keeps piling the money in.=E2=80=
=9D As Masa continued down the road with Neumann, there were few people ins=
ide SoftBank who were willing to challenge him, according to Barber, even t=
hough some believed Masa had to get out and that WeWork was not worth $47 b=
illion. =E2=80=9CMasa says, =E2=80=98That=E2=80=99s what everybody

=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Eventually, of course, Masa did establish =
his Vision Fund=E2=80=94which
has had mixed results=E2=80=94largely from investors in the Middle East, in=
cluding Saudi Arabia=E2=80=99s <strong>Mohammed bin Salman</strong>. =E2=80=
=9CThere=E2=80=99s a convergence of interest between Masa, a capital hungry=
 investor, and Mohammed bin Salman,=E2=80=9D Barber explained. M.B.S. was l=
ooking to move Saudi Arabia=E2=80=99s economy away from fossil fuels and to=
ward technological innovation, among other initiatives, as part of his so-c=
alled Vision 2030 plan. Masa was only too happy to oblige. =E2=80=9CMasa li=
kes to say=E2=80=94and he told
<strong>David Rubenstein</strong> this=E2=80=94=E2=80=98I persuaded M.B.S. =
to give me $45 billion in 45 minutes,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D said Barber. =E2=80=
=9CIn fact, there was=E2=80=94and I document this in the book=E2=80=94quite=
 a lot of diplomatic maneuvering =E2=80=A6 before they got the $45 billion.=
 And even when they did get the $45 billion, there was a lot of negotiation=
 about how it would be spent.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-botto=
m:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">In any event, over time, Masa beca=
me such an important symbolic figure in Japan that
the country=E2=80=99s establishment couldn=E2=80=99t let him fail=E2=80=94J=
apanese prestige became tied up in his success. <strong>Tadashi</strong> <s=
trong>Yanai</strong>, the founder of Uniqlo, who served on the SoftBank boa=
rd of directors for 18 years before his 2019 resignation, told Barber, in e=
ssence: =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98This guy was the icon. He was <em>the</em> dynami=
c entrepreneur. I thought I=E2=80=99d go on the board to help him and look =
after him a bit.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D The Japanese banks also gave him forbear=
ance, and he started again. =E2=80=9CHe pivots
to broadband, gets the money, takes on NTT=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94the incumbent t=
elecom provider in Japan=E2=80=94=E2=80=9Cand Bob=E2=80=99s your uncle.=E2=
=80=9D Masa is now worth somewhere between $20 billion and $30 billion, acc=
ording to the usual suspects.</p></div>

line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In the end, Barber managed to get four intervie=
ws with Masa,
totaling some nine hours of conversation. But he was careful to avoid embed=
ding with him=E2=80=94not that the secretive Masa would have allowed that=
=E2=80=94and to get what he needed while keeping his distance. (He also int=
erviewed about 150 other people.) He did, however, gain entrance to Masa=E2=
=80=99s private sanctum on the 37th floor of a Tokyo skyscraper, where Barb=
er had heard he presented his guests with sushi that =E2=80=9Csome people s=
ay [costs] $30,000 or so,=E2=80=9D Barber said. =E2=80=9CSo, I=E2=80=99m th=
inking I=E2=80=99ve got to find a way of
somehow getting into the inner sanctum to see this.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-b=
ottom:0px;">But he didn=E2=80=99t want to <em>ask</em> Masa, lest he owe a =
debt to the subject of his book. In the end, Masa brought it up himself at =
the end of an interview. =E2=80=9CWe shook hands, and then he said, =E2=80=
=98I want to show you something,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Barber recalled. =E2=80=
=9CAnd he opened these doors, and there is this Xanadu meets Kyoto. There a=
re three rock pools =E2=80=A6 and they=E2=80=99re
perfectly designed. There are trees with leaves painted individually, with =
gold tint. It=E2=80=99s an extraordinary thing, plus Japanese art. And, of =
course, <em>of course</em>, the Domaine de la Roman=C3=A9e-Conti wine, whic=
h is also $8,000 a bottle or so. There=E2=80=99s four bottles there by the =
dining table.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>Barber continued: =E2=80=9CHe took me round=
 there, and then he said, =E2=80=98You can=E2=80=99t write a word about thi=
s.=E2=80=99 I=E2=80=99m thinking, =E2=80=98What!?=E2=80=99 So I said, =E2=
=80=98Why not?=E2=80=99 And he said, =E2=80=98Because no journalist has eve=
r been in
this sanctum.=E2=80=99 And I then, without batting an eyelid, said, =E2=80=
=98But I=E2=80=99m not a journalist. I=E2=80=99m an author.=E2=80=99 Just k=
ind of cheeky, but I got away with it.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;"><=
br/>In any case, he thinks Masa liked the book well enough=E2=80=94the two =
ran into each other at the Allen & Co. conference in Sun Valley last su=
mmer and chatted; Barber introduced him to his wife. Masa did ask why Barbe=
r had called the book <em>Gambling Man</em>. Then he seemed to come around,=
 musing aloud: =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98Gambling Man, Gambling
Man. I suppose I am Gambling Man. I suppose I am. Yeah, okay.=E2=80=99=E2=
=80=9D</p></div>
